lem:feasible-drift-product-bound.proof_tex: Proof. Fix q in (0,1/2], a measurable plug-in nuisance triple bar_eta=(bar_mu_0,bar_mu_1,bar_e), and write bar_e_q(x)=min{1-q,max{q,bar_e(x)}}. Let Delta_a=bar_mu_a-mu_a and b_q(x)=E_P[Gamma_q(O;bar_eta)|X=x]-tau_P(x). By lem:clip-bias, b_q(x)=(bar_e_q(x)-e_P(x)) (Delta_1(x)/bar_e_q(x)+Delta_0(x)/(1-bar_e_q(x))). Since q <= bar_e_q <= 1-q, both denominators are at least q. Let D_pi={x: pi(x) != pi_star(x)}. The multiplier pi-pi_star is zero outside D_pi and has absolute value at most one, so Cauchy-Schwarz gives |E_P[(pi(X)-pi_star(X)) b_q(X)]| <= P_X(D_pi)^{1/2} ||b_q||_{L2(P_X)}. The displayed identity and the denominator lower bound give ||b_q||_{L2(P_X)} <= q^{-1} ||bar_e_q-e_P||_{L2(P_X)} (||Delta_1||_{L2(P_X)}+||Delta_0||_{L2(P_X)}). Under ass:nuisance-rate, the two regression-error norms are bounded by r_mu_n, up to an absolute factor, hence |E_P[(pi-pi_star)b_q]| <= C q^{-1} P_X(D_pi)^{1/2} r_mu_n ||bar_e_q-e_P||_2. Finally, by the triangle inequality, ||bar_e_q-e_P||_2 <= ||bar_e-e_P||_2+||bar_e_q-bar_e||_2 <= r_e_n+||bar_e_q-bar_e||_2. This proves the asserted reduction. Notice that no step identifies the support of bar_e_q-bar_e with the true weak-overlap set {p_P<=q}; that is precisely the remaining uncontrolled term.

Under ass:nuisance-rate this is at most C q^{-1} P_X(D_pi)^{1/2} r_mu_n (r_e_n+||bar_e_q-bar_e||_2).

lem:feasible-master-bound.proof_tex: Condition on the nuisance-training folds. Let b_q(x)=E_P[Gamma_q(O;etahat)|X=x]-tau_P(x). The ERM property gives P_n[(pi_star-pi_hat_n)Gamma_q(etahat)]<=0, hence R_P(pi_hat_n)<= (P-P_n)[(pi_star-pi_hat_n)Gamma_q(etahat)] + |P[(pi_star-pi_hat_n)b_q]|. The first term is controlled by ass:vc-localized-envelope. Because the q-clipped AIPW score has conditional second moment bounded by C/q on each disagreement set, the localized empirical term is at most C n^{-1/2}q^{-1/2}P_X(D_pi_hat_n)^{1/2}polylog(n). For the second term, lem:clip-bias and Cauchy-Schwarz imply |P[(pi-pi_star)b_q]| <= C q^{-1} r_mu,n ||ehat_q-e_P||_2 P_X(D_pi)^{1/2}. Lem:true-clipped-propensity-envelope gives q^{-1}r_mu,n||ehat_q-e_P||_2 <= C{r_mu,n r_e,n/q + 1_{gamma>0}r_mu,n q^{1/(2gamma)}}. Applying this with pi=pi_hat_n and using thm:margin-localization, P_X(D_pi_hat_n)^{1/2}<=C R_P(pi_hat_n)^{alpha/(2+2alpha)}. Thus R_P(pi_hat_n)<=C H_n(q) R_P(pi_hat_n)^{alpha/(2+2alpha)}, with H_n(q)=n^{-1/2}q^{-1/2}polylog(n)+r_mu,n r_e,n/q+1_{gamma>0}r_mu,n q^{1/(2gamma)}. Solving this self-bounding inequality gives R_P(pi_hat_n)<=C H_n(q)^{2(1+alpha)/(2+alpha)}. Integrating over the cross-fitting folds proves the lemma.

lem:clip-balance-exponent.proof_tex: For q_n=n^{-lambda}, the variance term, doubly robust product term, and true-clipping displacement term in lem:feasible-master-bound have exponents b_1(lambda)=1/2-lambda/2, b_2(lambda)=c-lambda, and b_3(lambda)=a+lambda/(2gamma). Thus the bracket has exponent min{b_1,b_2,b_3}, and maximizing this minimum gives zeta. The lower envelope of b_1 and b_2 switches where b_1=b_2, namely at lambda_0=2c-1. Since c>=1/2, lambda_0>=0. If a>=1/2, then at lambda=0 all three exponents are at least 1/2 and b_1(0)=1/2; any positive lambda decreases min{b_1,b_2}, so lambda_*=0 and zeta=1/2. Now suppose a<1/2. Balancing b_3 with b_1 gives lambda_1=gamma(1-2a)/(1+gamma) and value [1+2a gamma]/[2(1+gamma)]. This balance occurs before the switch, lambda_1<=lambda_0, exactly when c>=[1+2gamma(1-a)]/[2(1+gamma)]. In that case the optimum is lambda_1. If this condition fails, the lower envelope is b_2 when b_3 reaches it. Balancing b_3 with b_2 gives lambda_2=2gamma(c-a)/(2gamma+1) and value a+(c-a)/(2gamma+1)=(2gamma a+c)/(2gamma+1). The complementary condition c<=[1+2gamma(1-a)]/[2(1+gamma)] is exactly lambda_2>=lambda_0, so the optimum is lambda_2. The two formulae agree on the boundary.

snapshot.stmt: For gamma>0, if r_mu,n=O(n^{-a}) and r_mu,n r_e,n=O(n^{-c}) with c>=1/2, then the optimal polynomial clip q_n=n^{-lambda_*} for the master bound has exponent zeta(a,c,gamma)=sup_{lambda>=0} min{1/2-lambda/2,c-lambda,a+lambda/(2gamma)}. If a>=1/2, lambda_*=0 and zeta=1/2. If a<1/2 and c>=[1+2 gamma(1-a)]/[2(1+gamma)], lambda_*=gamma(1-2a)/(1+gamma) and zeta=[1+2a gamma]/[2(1+gamma)]. If a<1/2 and c<=[1+2 gamma(1-a)]/[2(1+gamma)], lambda_*=2 gamma(c-a)/(2 gamma+1) and zeta=(2 gamma a+c)/(2 gamma+1).

lem:feasible-drift-global-bound.proof_tex: By lem:clip-bias, \(b_q=(\bar e_q-e_P)(\Delta_1/\bar e_q+\Delta_0/(1-\bar e_q))\). Since \(q\le \bar e_q\le1-q\), both denominators are at least \(q\). Also \(|\pi-\pi_\star|\le1\). Hence Cauchy-Schwarz over \(P_X\) gives \(|E[(\pi-\pi_\star)b_q]|\le E|b_q|\le q^{-1}\|\bar e_q-e_P\|_2(\|\Delta_1\|_2+\|\Delta_0\|_2)\). For \(\bar\eta=\widehat\eta\), ass:nuisance-rate gives \(\|\Delta_0\|_2+\|\Delta_1\|_2\le Cr_{\mu,n}\) and \(\|\widehat e-e_P\|_2\le r_{e,n}\). Lem:true-clipped-propensity-envelope gives \(\|\widehat e_q-e_P\|_2\le r_{e,n}+Cq^{1+1/(2\gamma)}\) for \(\gamma>0\), and \(\|\widehat e_q-e_P\|_2\le r_{e,n}\) for \(\gamma=0\) when \(q\le\underline p\). Substitution proves the stated bounds.

snapshot.stmt: For \(q\in(0,1/2]\), any measurable plug-in nuisance triple \(\bar\eta=(\bar\mu_0,\bar\mu_1,\bar e)\), and \(b_q(x)=E_P[\Gamma_q(O;\bar\eta)\mid X=x]-\tau_P(x)\), every deterministic policy \(\pi\) satisfies \(|E_P[(\pi(X)-\pi_\star(X))b_q(X)]|\le q^{-1}\|\bar e_q-e_P\|_2(\|\Delta_1\|_2+\|\Delta_0\|_2)\). Consequently, for \(\bar\eta=\widehat\eta\), ass:nuisance-rate and lem:true-clipped-propensity-envelope imply the uniform bound \(|E_P[(\pi-\pi_\star)b_q]|\le C\{r_{\mu,n}r_{e,n}/q+1\{\gamma>0\}r_{\mu,n}q^{1/(2\gamma)}\}\), with the last term absent at \(\gamma=0\) under the strict-overlap endpoint and \(q\le\underline p\).

assumptions.ass:strict-overlap-endpoint: When gamma=0, p_P(X) >= underline_p holds P_X-almost surely, for a constant underline_p in (0,1/2].

lem:feasible-additive-master-bound.proof_tex: Condition on the nuisance-training folds. By the ERM property in def:feasible-erm, \(P_n[(\pi_\star-\widehat\pi_n)\Gamma_q(\widehat\eta)]\le0\). Adding and subtracting population means gives \(R_P(\widehat\pi_n)\le (P-P_n)[(\pi_\star-\widehat\pi_n)\Gamma_q(\widehat\eta)]+|P[(\pi_\star-\widehat\pi_n)b_q]|\). The clipped AIPW score has conditional second moment on a disagreement set \(D\) bounded by \(Cq^{-1}P_X(D)\), because outcomes and true regressions are bounded and the clipped denominators are at least \(q\). Assumption ass:vc-localized-envelope therefore bounds the expected localized empirical term by \(C n^{-1/2}q^{-1/2}P_X(D_{\widehat\pi_n})^{1/2}(\log n)^{p_0}\). Lem:feasible-drift-global-bound bounds the drift term by \(B_n(q)=C\{r_{\mu,n}r_{e,n}/q+1\{\gamma>0\}r_{\mu,n}q^{1/(2\gamma)}\}\). By thm:margin-localization, \(P_X(D_{\widehat\pi_n})^{1/2}\le C R_P(\widehat\pi_n)^{\alpha/(2+2\alpha)}\). Thus \(R_P(\widehat\pi_n)\le C A_n(q)R_P(\widehat\pi_n)^s+B_n(q)\), where \(A_n(q)=n^{-1/2}q^{-1/2}(\log n)^{p_0}\) and \(s=\alpha/(2+2\alpha)\). If \(r\le ar^s+b\) with \(0\le s<1\), then either \(r\le 2b\) or \(r/2\le ar^s\), giving \(r\le (2a)^{1/(1-s)}+2b\). Since \(1/(1-s)=2(1+\alpha)/(2+\alpha)=\kappa\), the asserted bound follows after integrating over the training folds.

At \(\gamma=0\), under ass:strict-overlap-endpoint and \(q\le\underline p\), the last term is absent.

lem:additive-clip-balance-exponent.proof_tex: For \(q_n=n^{-\lambda}\), the three terms in lem:feasible-additive-master-bound have exponents \(A(\lambda)=r_0(1-\lambda)\), \(B(\lambda)=c-\lambda\), and \(C(\lambda)=a+\lambda/(2\gamma)\). The function \(\min\{A,B,C\}\) is the minimum of affine functions, so its maximum occurs at \(\lambda=0\) or where the increasing line \(C\) meets the lower envelope of the two decreasing lines \(A\) and \(B\). If \(a\ge\min\{r_0,c\}\), then \(C(0)\) is not below the decreasing envelope at the origin, so \(\lambda=0\) is optimal and the value is \(\min\{r_0,c\}\). Now assume \(a<\min\{r_0,c\}\). If \(c<r_0\), then \(B\) is already below \(A\) at the origin and remains below because it has the steeper negative slope, so the optimum is at \(B=C\), namely \(\lambda=2\gamma(c-a)/(1+2\gamma)\) and value \((c+2\gamma a)/(1+2\gamma)\). If \(c\ge r_0\), the lower decreasing envelope is first \(A\), then \(B\) after their crossing. The intersection \(A=C\) is \(\lambda=2\gamma(r_0-a)/(1+2\gamma r_0)\), with value \(r_0(1+2\gamma a)/(1+2\gamma r_0)\). This point is feasible before \(B\) becomes binding exactly when \(B\) at that point is at least this value, equivalently \(c\ge T_A=[r_0(1+2\gamma a)+2\gamma(r_0-a)]/(1+2\gamma r_0)\). In that case it is optimal. If \(c<T_A\), \(B\) becomes binding first, and the optimum is the \(B=C\) value just computed. These cases exhaust all possibilities.

snapshot.stmt: Let \(\gamma>0\), \(r_0=(1+\alpha)/(2+\alpha)\), and suppose \(r_{\mu,n}=O(n^{-a})\), \(r_{\mu,n}r_{e,n}=O(n^{-c})\) with \(c\ge1/2\). For the additive master bound, the polynomial clip \(q_n=\min\{1/4,n^{-\lambda_\star}\}\) has optimized raw exponent \(\widetilde r_{feas}=\sup_{\lambda\ge0}\min\{r_0(1-\lambda),c-\lambda,a+\lambda/(2\gamma)\}\). A closed-form optimizer is as follows. If \(a\ge\min\{r_0,c\}\), take \(\lambda_\star=0\) and \(\widetilde r_{feas}=\min\{r_0,c\}\). If \(a<\min\{r_0,c\}\) and either \(c<r_0\) or \(c<T_A\), take \(\lambda_\star=2\gamma(c-a)/(1+2\gamma)\) and \(\widetilde r_{feas}=(c+2\gamma a)/(1+2\gamma)\). If \(a<r_0\) and \(c\ge T_A\), take \(\lambda_\star=2\gamma(r_0-a)/(1+2\gamma r_0)\) and \(\widetilde r_{feas}=r_0(1+2\gamma a)/(1+2\gamma r_0)\), where \(T_A=[r_0(1+2\gamma a)+2\gamma(r_0-a)]/(1+2\gamma r_0)\).

lem:feasible-attainment-at-equal-exponen.proof_tex:
By thm:rate-characterization, there are constants c,C,p>0 such that, for all sufficiently large n,
\[
  c n^{-r_\star(\alpha,\gamma)}\le M_n(\alpha,\gamma)\le C n^{-r_{m feas}(\alpha,\gamma)}(\log n)^p,
\]
and the displayed upper bound is attained by the cross-fitted clipped-AIPW ERM of def:feasible-erm with the clip chosen in oeq:feasible-upper. Suppose now that the parameter and nuisance-rate regime satisfies \(r_{m feas}(\alpha,\gamma)=r_\star(\alpha,\gamma)\). Substituting this equality in the upper inequality gives
\[
  \sup_{P\in {\cal P}_{\alpha,\gamma}} E_P R_P(\widehat\pi_n)\le C n^{-r_\star(\alpha,\gamma)}(\log n)^p .
\]
Since the lower inequality holds for the minimax risk over all measurable \(\Pi\)-valued estimators, no estimator can improve on the polynomial exponent \(r_\star(\alpha,\gamma)\). Hence the feasible cross-fitted clipped-AIPW ERM attains the converse exponent up to logarithmic factors. At the strict-overlap endpoint \(\gamma=0\), def:exponents gives \( eta_{\alpha,0}=0\) and thm:rate-characterization states \(r_{m feas}=r_\star=(1+\alpha)/(2+\alpha)\), so the same conclusion applies there.

snapshot.stmt: Under thm:rate-characterization, in every parameter and nuisance-rate regime where r_feas(alpha,gamma)=r_star(alpha,gamma), the cross-fitted clipped-AIPW ERM of def:feasible-erm attains the converse welfare-regret exponent r_star(alpha,gamma) up to logarithmic factors. In particular this holds at the strict-overlap endpoint gamma=0, where r_star=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha).

defs.def:feasible-erm: Vhat_{n,q}(pi) = n^{-1} sum_i pi(X_i) Gamma_q(O_i; etahat^{(-k(i))}) with K-fold cross-fitted nuisances etahat and clip q in (0,1/2]; pi_hat_n in argmax_{pi in Pi} Vhat_{n,q}(pi). The clip q is the analysis tuning parameter (chosen by the bias-variance balance).
defs.def:exponents: beta_ag = (0 if gamma = 0 else alpha gamma/(alpha+1)); D_ag = 2 + alpha + beta_ag; r_star(alpha,gamma) = (1+alpha)/D_ag.

thm:welfare-identity.proof_tex:
By def:welfare-regret,
\[
R_P(\pi)=V_P(\pi_\star)-V_P(\pi)=E_P\bigl[(\pi_\star(X)-\pi(X))\tau_P(X)\bigr].
\]
Fix \(x\in X\). If \(\tau_P(x)>0\), then by definition \(\pi_\star(x)=1\), so
\[
(\pi_\star(x)-\pi(x))\tau_P(x)=(1-\pi(x))\tau_P(x)=|\tau_P(x)|\,1\{\pi(x)\neq \pi_\star(x)\}.
\]
If \(\tau_P(x)<0\), then \(\pi_\star(x)=0\), so
\[
(\pi_\star(x)-\pi(x))\tau_P(x)=-\pi(x)\tau_P(x)=|\tau_P(x)|\,1\{\pi(x)\neq \pi_\star(x)\}.
\]
If \(\tau_P(x)=0\), both sides are \(0\). Hence the pointwise identity
\[
(\pi_\star(x)-\pi(x))\tau_P(x)=|\tau_P(x)|\,1\{\pi(x)\neq \pi_\star(x)\}
\]
holds for every \(x\). Evaluating at \(X\) and taking expectations gives
\[
R_P(\pi)=E_P\bigl[|\tau_P(X)|\,1\{\pi(X)\neq \pi_\star(X)\}\bigr].
\]
Because ass:bounded-outcome implies \(\mu_a(X)\in[-1,1]\) and therefore \(|\tau_P(X)|\le 2\), the integrand is bounded and measurable as a function of \(X\) alone. Therefore its expectation is its integral against the covariate marginal:
\[
E_P\bigl[|\tau_P(X)|\,1\{\pi(X)\neq \pi_\star(X)\}\bigr]
=\int_X |\tau_P(x)|\,1\{\pi(x)\neq \pi_\star(x)\}\,dP_X(x).
\]
This proves both displayed equalities.

snapshot.stmt: Under ass:iid and ass:bounded-outcome, for every deterministic pi in Pi, R_P(pi) = E_P[ |tau_P(X)| 1{pi(X) != pi_star(X)} ] = integral over X of |tau_P(x)| 1{pi(x) != pi_star(x)} dP_X(x).

defs.def:welfare-regret: V_P(pi) = E_P[pi(X) tau_P(X)]; pi_star(x) = 1{tau_P(x) >= 0}; R_P(pi) = V_P(pi_star) - V_P(pi).

thm:margin-localization.proof_tex:
Let \(D_\pi=\{x:\pi(x)\neq \pi_\star(x)\}\). By ass:zero-effect, for every deterministic \(\pi\in\Pi\),
\[
P_X\bigl(D_\pi\cap\{\tau_P=0\}\bigr)=0.
\]
We first isolate the corner case \(R_P(\pi)=0\). By thm:welfare-identity,
\[
0=R_P(\pi)=E_P\bigl[|\tau_P(X)|1_{D_\pi}(X)\bigr].
\]
The integrand is nonnegative, so \(|\tau_P(X)|1_{D_\pi}(X)=0\) almost surely. Hence
\[
P_X\bigl(D_\pi\cap\{|\tau_P|>0\}\bigr)=0.
\]
Combining this with \(P_X(D_\pi\cap\{\tau_P=0\})=0\) yields \(P_X(D_\pi)=0\). Therefore
\[
P_X(D_\pi)=0\le C\,R_P(\pi)^{\alpha/(1+\alpha)}
\]
for every \(C>0\), so the claim is true when \(R_P(\pi)=0\).

Now assume \(R_P(\pi)>0\). Fix any \(u\in(0,u_0]\). Since \(D_\pi\subseteq \{0<|\tau_P|\le u\}\cup(D_\pi\cap\{|\tau_P|>u\})\cup(D_\pi\cap\{\tau_P=0\})\) and the last set has zero \(P_X\)-measure by ass:zero-effect,
\[
P_X(D_\pi)
\le P\bigl(0<|\tau_P(X)|\le u\bigr)+P\bigl(D_\pi\cap\{|\tau_P(X)|>u\}\bigr).
\]
Assumption ass:margin bounds the first term by \(C_m u^\alpha\). On the second term we have \(|\tau_P|>u\), so
\[
u\,P\bigl(D_\pi\cap\{|\tau_P(X)|>u\}\bigr)
\le E_P\bigl[|\tau_P(X)|1_{D_\pi}(X)\bigr]
=R_P(\pi)
\]
by thm:welfare-identity. Therefore, for every \(u\in(0,u_0]\),
\[
P_X(D_\pi)\le C_m u^\alpha+\frac{R_P(\pi)}{u}.
\]
If \(0<R_P(\pi)\le u_0^{1+\alpha}\), choose \(u=R_P(\pi)^{1/(1+\alpha)}\in(0,u_0]\). Then
\[
P_X(D_\pi)
\le C_m R_P(\pi)^{\alpha/(1+\alpha)}+R_P(\pi)^{\alpha/(1+\alpha)}
=(C_m+1)R_P(\pi)^{\alpha/(1+\alpha)}.
\]
If instead \(R_P(\pi)>u_0^{1+\alpha}\), then trivially \(P_X(D_\pi)\le 1\), while
\[
1\le u_0^{-\alpha} R_P(\pi)^{\alpha/(1+\alpha)}.
\]
Hence in this regime,
\[
P_X(D_\pi)\le u_0^{-\alpha} R_P(\pi)^{\alpha/(1+\alpha)}.
\]
Combining the three cases \(R_P(\pi)=0\), \(0<R_P(\pi)\le u_0^{1+\alpha}\), and \(R_P(\pi)>u_0^{1+\alpha}\), we obtain
\[
P_X(D_\pi)\le C\,R_P(\pi)^{\alpha/(1+\alpha)},
\qquad C:=(C_m+1)+u_0^{-\alpha}.
\]
This is the claimed localization inequality.

snapshot.stmt: Under ass:margin and ass:zero-effect there is C=C(C_m,u_0,alpha) such that for every pi in Pi, P_X(D_pi) <= C R_P(pi)^(alpha/(1+alpha)).

assumptions.ass:margin: P(0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u) <= C_m u^alpha for all 0 < u <= u_0.
assumptions.ass:zero-effect: Either P_X{tau_P(X)=0}=0, or every pi in Pi agrees with pi_star P_X-almost everywhere on {tau_P(X)=0}.

prop:overlap-envelope.proof_tex: Substitute the tight-window choice u=h^(beta/gamma) and v=h^beta. Then u^alpha v^(1/gamma)=h^(alpha beta/gamma) h^(beta/gamma)=h^(((alpha+1) beta)/gamma). Because 0<h<1, the inequality h^(((alpha+1) beta)/gamma) >= h^alpha is equivalent to ((alpha+1) beta)/gamma <= alpha, i.e. beta <= alpha gamma/(alpha+1). Equality holds exactly at beta=beta_ag. For a block of mass of order h^alpha, contrast of order h, and weak-arm propensity of order h^beta, the event {p_P(X) <= v, 0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u} contains the block as soon as u is large enough to include the contrast level and v is large enough to include the weak-arm propensity level; the tight admissibility check is therefore exactly the displayed exponent comparison. Hence the overlap-decay envelope admits such a block iff beta <= beta_ag, and the least informative admissible choice is beta=beta_ag.

snapshot.stmt: For alpha >= 0, gamma > 0, h in (0,1), beta >= 0, at the tight window v=h^beta, u=h^(beta/gamma): u^alpha v^(1/gamma) = h^((alpha+1)beta/gamma), and u^alpha v^(1/gamma) >= h^alpha iff beta <= alpha gamma/(alpha+1), with equality at beta = beta_ag. Hence a block of mass ~ h^alpha, contrast ~ h, propensity ~ h^beta meets the ass:overlap-decay envelope iff beta <= beta_ag, and beta_ag is the least informative admissible weak-arm exponent.

defs.def:exponents: beta_ag = (0 if gamma = 0 else alpha gamma/(alpha+1)); D_ag = 2 + alpha + beta_ag; r_star(alpha,gamma) = (1+alpha)/D_ag.

assumptions.ass:overlap-decay: P{p_P(X) <= v, 0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u} <= C_o u^alpha v^(1/gamma) for all 0 < v <= c_o u^gamma, with v^(1/gamma)=1 when gamma=0.

lem:witness-membership.proof_tex:
Write $m_n:=P_X(B_n)=c_B h_n^{\alpha}$ and $q_n:=1/4$ when $\beta_{ag}=0$, $q_n:=h_n^{\beta_{ag}}$ otherwise. By ass:margin-window, $u_0<2$, so we may choose once and for all an off-block contrast level $\tau_0\in(u_0,2)$. Because $h_n\to0$, for all large $n$ we also have $h_n\le u_0$ and $q_n\in(0,1/2]$.

Construct the witness law explicitly as follows. Let $X\sim\mathrm{Unif}[0,1]$, let $B_n=[0,c_B h_n^{\alpha}]$, and on $B_n$ let $Y(0)\in\{-1,1\}$ have mean $0$ while $Y(1)\in\{-1,1\}$ has mean $\sigma h_n$ under $P_{n,\sigma}$, $\sigma\in\{+,-\}$. Off $B_n$, choose a common $\{-1,1\}$-valued potential-outcome law with contrast $\tau_0>u_0$ and treatment $1$ optimal. Draw $A\mid X\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(q_n)$ on $B_n$ and $A\mid X\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(1/2)$ on $B_n^c$, independently of $(Y(0),Y(1))$ given $X$, and set $Y=AY(1)+(1-A)Y(0)$.

From this construction, ass:iid holds because $O_1,\dots,O_n$ are i.i.d. draws from the same law. Ass:consistency holds by the defining relation $Y=AY(1)+(1-A)Y(0)$. Ass:unconfoundedness holds because $(Y(0),Y(1))\perp A\mid X$ by construction. Ass:bounded-outcome holds because $Y(a)\in\{-1,1\}\subset[-1,1]$. Ass:positivity holds because on $B_n$ the propensity equals $q_n\in(0,1/2]$, while off $B_n$ it equals $1/2$.

The induced contrast is
\[
\tau_{P_{n,\sigma}}(x)=\sigma h_n\quad\text{for }x\in B_n,
\qquad
\tau_{P_{n,\sigma}}(x)=\tau_0\quad\text{for }x\notin B_n.
\]
Hence $\tau_{P_{n,\sigma}}$ never vanishes, so ass:zero-effect holds by its first alternative, namely $P_X\{\tau_{P_{n,\sigma}}(X)=0\}=0$.

We next verify ass:margin. Fix $0<u\le u_0$. Because $\tau_0>u_0$, the set $\{0<|\tau_{P_{n,\sigma}}(X)|\le u\}$ cannot intersect $B_n^c$. If $u<h_n$, the set is empty. If $u\ge h_n$, the set equals $B_n$. Therefore
\[
P\{0<|\tau_{P_{n,\sigma}}(X)|\le u\}
\in\{0,m_n\}
\le c_B h_n^{\alpha}
\le c_B u^{\alpha}.
\]
Choosing the free witness constant $c_B\le C_m$ yields ass:margin.

Now verify ass:overlap-decay.

Case 1: $\gamma=0$. Then $\beta_{ag}=0$ and $q_n=1/4$. If $v<1/4$, the event $\{p_P(X)\le v,\ 0<|\tau_P(X)|\le u\}$ is empty because $p_P=1/4$ on $B_n$ and $p_P=1/2$ off $B_n$. If $v\ge1/4$ but $u<h_n$, it is still empty. If $v\ge1/4$ and $u\ge h_n$, the event is exactly $B_n$, so its probability is $m_n\le c_B u^{\alpha}$. Since for $\gamma=0$ the factor $v^{1/\gamma}$ is interpreted as $1$, this is the required bound after shrinking $c_B$ so that $c_B\le C_o$.

Case 2: $\gamma>0$ and $\beta_{ag}=0$. By def:exponents this means $\alpha=0$, and again $q_n=1/4$. Exactly as above, the overlap event is empty unless simultaneously $u\ge h_n$ and $v\ge1/4$; in the nonempty case its probability is $m_n=c_B$. Since then $u^{\alpha}=1$ and $v^{1/\gamma}\ge4^{-1/\gamma}$,
\[
P\{p_P(X)\le v,\ 0<|\tau_P(X)|\le u\}=c_B
\le C_o 4^{-1/\gamma}
\le C_o u^{\alpha} v^{1/\gamma}
\]
after one more harmless restriction on $c_B$.

Case 3: $\gamma>0$ and $\beta_{ag}>0$. On $B_n$ we have $p_P=q_n=h_n^{\beta_{ag}}$, while on $B_n^c$ we have $p_P=1/2$ and $|\tau_P|=\tau_0>u_0$. Hence any overlap-decay event can only come from $B_n$. If the event is nonempty, then necessarily $u\ge h_n$ and $v\ge h_n^{\beta_{ag}}$. Because ass:overlap-decay is required only on the range $0<v\le c_o u^{\gamma}$, nonemptiness also implies
\[
u\ge c_o^{-1/\gamma} v^{1/\gamma}
\ge c_o^{-1/\gamma} h_n^{\beta_{ag}/\gamma}.
\]
Therefore
\[
u^{\alpha} v^{1/\gamma}
\ge c_o^{-\alpha/\gamma} h_n^{\alpha\beta_{ag}/\gamma} h_n^{\beta_{ag}/\gamma}
= c_o^{-\alpha/\gamma} h_n^{((\alpha+1)\beta_{ag})/\gamma}.
\]
By def:exponents, $\beta_{ag}=\alpha\gamma/(\alpha+1)$ when $\gamma>0$, so the exponent on the right is exactly $\alpha$. Hence $u^{\alpha}v^{1/\gamma}\ge c h_n^{\alpha}$ for a constant $c>0$ depending only on $c_o,\alpha,\gamma$. Because the event is contained in $B_n$,
\[
P\{p_P(X)\le v,\ 0<|\tau_P(X)|\le u\}
\le m_n=c_B h_n^{\alpha}
\le C_o u^{\alpha} v^{1/\gamma}
\]
after choosing $c_B$ small relative to $C_o c$.

Thus for all large $n$ the witness laws $P_{n,+}$ and $P_{n,-}$ satisfy ass:iid, ass:consistency, ass:unconfoundedness, ass:bounded-outcome, ass:positivity, ass:margin, ass:zero-effect, and ass:overlap-decay. Finally, the optimal policy under $P_{n,+}$ is the treat-all policy $x\mapsto1$, because the contrast is positive everywhere; the optimal policy under $P_{n,-}$ is $x\mapsto1\{x\notin B_n\}$, because the contrast equals $-h_n$ on $B_n$ and $\tau_0>0$ off $B_n$. If these two explicit witness-optimal policies belong to $\Pi$, then ass:optimal-in-class also holds for the two witnesses. Together with ass:policy-class, this places both laws in def:law-class $P_{class}(\alpha,\gamma)$.

snapshot.stmt: For all large n the corrected witness laws P_{n,+}, P_{n,-} satisfy ass:iid, ass:consistency, ass:unconfoundedness, ass:bounded-outcome, ass:positivity, ass:margin, ass:zero-effect, and ass:overlap-decay. If, in addition, the two explicit witness-optimal policies x -> 1 and x -> 1{x notin B_n} belong to Pi, then P_{n,+}, P_{n,-} lie in def:law-class P_class(alpha,gamma). (Normalization: the margin window satisfies u_0<2, so an off-block contrast tau_0 in (u_0, 2) exists under bounded outcomes |Y|<=1.)

defs.def:two-point-witness: On X=[0,1] with P_X=Lebesgue, fix h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)}, q_n=(1/4 if beta_ag=0 else h_n^{beta_ag}), and an active block B_n=[0, c_B h_n^alpha] with P_X(B_n)=c_B h_n^alpha. The two laws P_{n,sigma}, sigma in {+,-}, share P_X, share the logging propensity e(x)=q_n on B_n and e(x)=1/2 off B_n, and share an off-block outcome law with common optimal label 1 and contrast tau_0 > u_0. On B_n set mu_0(x)=0 and let the treated outcome on the active treated cell {X in B_n, A=1} satisfy P(Y=1 | X in B_n, A=1)=(1+sigma h_n)/2, so tau_{P_{n,sigma}}=sigma h_n on B_n. The laws agree everywhere off the treated active cell. Constants are chosen with 8 C_B C_Q < log 5.
defs.def:law-class: { P : ass:iid, ass:consistency, ass:unconfoundedness, ass:bounded-outcome, ass:positivity, ass:margin, ass:zero-effect, ass:overlap-decay, ass:policy-class, and ass:optimal-in-class hold at exponents (alpha,gamma) }

assumptions.ass:overlap-decay: P{p_P(X) <= v, 0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u} <= C_o u^alpha v^(1/gamma) for all 0 < v <= c_o u^gamma, with v^(1/gamma)=1 when gamma=0.
assumptions.ass:margin: P(0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u) <= C_m u^alpha for all 0 < u <= u_0.

lem:two-point-divergence.proof_tex:
Let $E_n:=\{X\in B_n,\ A=1\}$. Under def:two-point-witness, the laws $P_{n,+}$ and $P_{n,-}$ have the same marginal law of $X$, the same conditional law of $A$ given $X$, and the same conditional law of $Y$ everywhere except on $E_n$. The mass of that charged cell is
\[
P_{n,+}(E_n)=P_{n,-}(E_n)=P_X(B_n) q_n = m_n q_n,
\qquad m_n=c_B h_n^{\alpha}.
\]
Conditional on $E_n$, $Y\in\{-1,1\}$ and
\[
P_{n,+}(Y=1\mid E_n)=\frac{1+h_n}{2},
\qquad
P_{n,-}(Y=1\mid E_n)=\frac{1-h_n}{2}.
\]
Hence the conditional cellwise divergence is the chi-square divergence between two Bernoulli laws with parameters $(1+h_n)/2$ and $(1-h_n)/2$:
\[
\chi^2\!\left(\mathrm{Ber}\Bigl(\frac{1+h_n}{2}\Bigr)\Bigm\|\mathrm{Ber}\Bigl(\frac{1-h_n}{2}\Bigr)\right)
= \frac{h_n^2}{(1-h_n)/2}+\frac{h_n^2}{(1+h_n)/2}
= \frac{4 h_n^2}{1-h_n^2}
\le 8 h_n^2
\]
for all large $n$, because $h_n\to 0$.

Since the two laws coincide on $E_n^c$ and have the same mass $m_n q_n$ on $E_n$, disintegration over the partition $\{E_n,E_n^c\}$ gives
\[
\chi^2(P_{n,+}\|P_{n,-})
= P(E_n)\,\chi^2\!\left(P_{n,+}(\cdot\mid E_n)\middle\|P_{n,-}(\cdot\mid E_n)\right)
\le C m_n q_n h_n^2.
\]
Now $m_n\asymp h_n^{\alpha}$ by construction. Also $q_n\asymp h_n^{\beta_{ag}}$: if $\beta_{ag}>0$, this is exact because $q_n=h_n^{\beta_{ag}}$; if $\beta_{ag}=0$, then $q_n=1/4$ is a positive constant, hence still of order $h_n^0$. Therefore
\[
\chi^2(P_{n,+}\|P_{n,-}) \le C h_n^{2+\alpha+\beta_{ag}}.
\]
By def:two-point-witness, $h_n=n^{-1/(2+\alpha+\beta_{ag})}$, so
\[
n\,\chi^2(P_{n,+}\|P_{n,-}) \le C n h_n^{2+\alpha+\beta_{ag}} = C.
\]
For product laws, chi-square tensorizes multiplicatively:
\[
1+\chi^2(P_{n,+}^{\otimes n}\|P_{n,-}^{\otimes n})
= \bigl(1+\chi^2(P_{n,+}\|P_{n,-})\bigr)^n.
\]
Using $\log(1+t)\le t$ and the preceding bound,
\[
1+\chi^2(P_{n,+}^{\otimes n}\|P_{n,-}^{\otimes n})
\le \exp\!\bigl(n\chi^2(P_{n,+}\|P_{n,-})\bigr)
\le e^C,
\]
so the $n$-fold divergence is uniformly bounded. This is exactly the claimed estimate.

snapshot.stmt: For the def:two-point-witness laws, the per-observation chi-square is chi^2(P_{n,+} || P_{n,-}) <= C m_n q_n h_n^2 with m_n=P_X(B_n)~h_n^alpha and q_n~h_n^{beta_ag}, hence ~ h_n^{2+alpha+beta_ag}; for h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)} the n-fold divergence is bounded, chi^2(P_{n,+}^{⊗n} || P_{n,-}^{⊗n}) <= C (equivalently 1+chi^2(P^{⊗n}) = (1+O(h_n^{2+alpha+beta_ag}))^n <= e^{C}).

defs.def:two-point-witness: On X=[0,1] with P_X=Lebesgue, fix h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)}, q_n=(1/4 if beta_ag=0 else h_n^{beta_ag}), and an active block B_n=[0, c_B h_n^alpha] with P_X(B_n)=c_B h_n^alpha. The two laws P_{n,sigma}, sigma in {+,-}, share P_X, share the logging propensity e(x)=q_n on B_n and e(x)=1/2 off B_n, and share an off-block outcome law with common optimal label 1 and contrast tau_0 > u_0. On B_n set mu_0(x)=0 and let the treated outcome on the active treated cell {X in B_n, A=1} satisfy P(Y=1 | X in B_n, A=1)=(1+sigma h_n)/2, so tau_{P_{n,sigma}}=sigma h_n on B_n. The laws agree everywhere off the treated active cell. Constants are chosen with 8 C_B C_Q < log 5.
defs.def:exponents: beta_ag = (0 if gamma = 0 else alpha gamma/(alpha+1)); D_ag = 2 + alpha + beta_ag; r_star(alpha,gamma) = (1+alpha)/D_ag.

lem:regret-separation.proof_tex:
Under def:two-point-witness, the two laws have the same positive off-block contrast \(\tau_0\), and on the active block \(B_n\) they have opposite contrasts:
\[
\tau_{P_{n,+}}(x)=h_n\quad\text{for }x\in B_n,
\qquad
\tau_{P_{n,-}}(x)=-h_n\quad\text{for }x\in B_n.
\]
Hence the optimal policy is \(\pi_{\star,+}=1\) on \(B_n\) under \(P_{n,+}\), whereas \(\pi_{\star,-}=0\) on \(B_n\) under \(P_{n,-}\). Fix any deterministic policy \(\pi\in\Pi\), and write
\[
S:=\{x\in B_n:\pi(x)=1\},
\qquad
B_n\setminus S=\{x\in B_n:\pi(x)=0\}.
\]
By thm:welfare-identity, the regret contributed by \(B_n\) under the plus law is
\[
R_{P_{n,+}}(\pi;B_n)
=\int_{B_n} |\tau_{P_{n,+}}(x)|1\{\pi(x)\neq \pi_{\star,+}(x)\}\,dP_X(x)
=h_n P_X(B_n\setminus S),
\]
because on \(B_n\) the policy disagrees with the plus-law optimum exactly where \(\pi=0\). Likewise, under the minus law,
\[
R_{P_{n,-}}(\pi;B_n)
=\int_{B_n} |\tau_{P_{n,-}}(x)|1\{\pi(x)\neq \pi_{\star,-}(x)\}\,dP_X(x)
=h_n P_X(S),
\]
because on \(B_n\) the policy disagrees with the minus-law optimum exactly where \(\pi=1\).

Off \(B_n\), both laws have the same positive contrast \(\tau_0\), so any disagreement loss there is nonnegative and can be dropped when taking a lower bound. Therefore
\[
R_{P_{n,+}}(\pi)\ge h_n P_X(B_n\setminus S),
\qquad
R_{P_{n,-}}(\pi)\ge h_n P_X(S).
\]
Taking the larger of the two gives
\[
\max\{R_{P_{n,+}}(\pi),R_{P_{n,-}}(\pi)\}
\ge h_n\max\{P_X(S),P_X(B_n\setminus S)\}.
\]
Since \(P_X(S)+P_X(B_n\setminus S)=P_X(B_n)\), we have
\[
\max\{P_X(S),P_X(B_n\setminus S)\}\ge \frac12 P_X(B_n).
\]
By def:two-point-witness, \(P_X(B_n)=c_B h_n^\alpha\). Hence
\[
\max\{R_{P_{n,+}}(\pi),R_{P_{n,-}}(\pi)\}
\ge \frac{c_B}{2} h_n^{1+\alpha}.
\]
This lower bound is uniform in \(\pi\). Taking the infimum over \(\pi\in\Pi\) yields
\[
\inf_{\pi\in\Pi}\max_{\sigma\in\{+,-\}}R_{P_{n,\sigma}}(\pi)
\ge c h_n^{1+\alpha}
\]
with \(c=c_B/2>0\).

snapshot.stmt: For the def:two-point-witness laws, inf_{pi in Pi} max_{sigma in {+,-}} R_{P_{n,sigma}}(pi) >= c h_n^(1+alpha): the optimal policies are opposite on B_n, so any pi misclassifies B_n under at least one law and pays regret >= (1/2) P_X(B_n) h_n ~ h_n^(1+alpha).

defs.def:two-point-witness: On X=[0,1] with P_X=Lebesgue, fix h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)}, q_n=(1/4 if beta_ag=0 else h_n^{beta_ag}), and an active block B_n=[0, c_B h_n^alpha] with P_X(B_n)=c_B h_n^alpha. The two laws P_{n,sigma}, sigma in {+,-}, share P_X, share the logging propensity e(x)=q_n on B_n and e(x)=1/2 off B_n, and share an off-block outcome law with common optimal label 1 and contrast tau_0 > u_0. On B_n set mu_0(x)=0 and let the treated outcome on the active treated cell {X in B_n, A=1} satisfy P(Y=1 | X in B_n, A=1)=(1+sigma h_n)/2, so tau_{P_{n,sigma}}=sigma h_n on B_n. The laws agree everywhere off the treated active cell. Constants are chosen with 8 C_B C_Q < log 5.

thm:minimax-lower.proof_tex:
Assume the two explicit witness-optimal policies \(x\mapsto 1\) and \(x\mapsto 1\{x\notin B_n\}\) belong to \(\Pi\) for all large \(n\). By lem:witness-membership, both witness laws \(P_{n,+}\) and \(P_{n,-}\) belong to \(P_{class}(\alpha,\gamma)\).

Fix any measurable \(\Pi\)-valued estimator \(\widehat\pi\) based on \(O_1,\dots,O_n\), and write
\[
P_+^n:=P_{n,+}^{\otimes n},
\qquad
P_-^n:=P_{n,-}^{\otimes n}.
\]
For each fixed \(x\in B_n\), define the measurable test
\[
T_x(O_1,\dots,O_n):=\widehat\pi(O_1,\dots,O_n)(x)\in\{0,1\}.
\]
Under \(P_{n,+}\), treatment \(1\) is optimal on \(B_n\); under \(P_{n,-}\), treatment \(0\) is optimal on \(B_n\). Therefore thm:welfare-identity gives
\[
E_{P_+^n}\bigl[R_{P_{n,+}}(\widehat\pi)\bigr]
\ge h_n\int_{B_n} P_+^n\{T_x=0\}\,dP_X(x),
\]
and
\[
E_{P_-^n}\bigl[R_{P_{n,-}}(\widehat\pi)\bigr]
\ge h_n\int_{B_n} P_-^n\{T_x=1\}\,dP_X(x).
\]
Adding the two inequalities yields
\[
E_{P_+^n}R_{P_{n,+}}(\widehat\pi)+E_{P_-^n}R_{P_{n,-}}(\widehat\pi)
\ge h_n\int_{B_n}\Bigl(P_+^n\{T_x=0\}+P_-^n\{T_x=1\}\Bigr)\,dP_X(x).
\]
For each fixed \(x\in B_n\), apply lem:bretagnolle-huber-testing to the test \(1-T_x\). This gives
\[
P_+^n\{T_x=0\}+P_-^n\{T_x=1\}
\ge \tfrac12 \exp\bigl(-\mathrm{KL}(P_+^n\|P_-^n)\bigr).
\]
It remains to bound the Kullback--Leibler divergence uniformly in \(n\). Let \(L=dP_+^n/dP_-^n\) on the set where \(P_+^n\ll P_-^n\). If absolute continuity failed then \(\chi^2(P_+^n\|P_-^n)=\infty\), so the inequality below is trivial; thus we may work on the absolutely continuous case. Since \(E_{P_-^n}[L]=1\), Jensen's inequality gives
\[
\mathrm{KL}(P_+^n\|P_-^n)
=E_{P_+^n}[\log L]
=E_{P_-^n}[L\log L]
\le \log E_{P_-^n}[L^2]
=\log\bigl(1+\chi^2(P_+^n\|P_-^n)\bigr)
\le \chi^2(P_+^n\|P_-^n),
\]
where the last step uses \(\log(1+t)\le t\) for \(t\ge 0\). By lem:two-point-divergence, there exists a constant \(C_0<\infty\), independent of \(n\), such that
\[
\chi^2(P_+^n\|P_-^n)\le C_0
\]
for all large \(n\). Hence also
\[
\mathrm{KL}(P_+^n\|P_-^n)\le C_0
\]
for all large \(n\). Substituting into the testing bound yields the uniform error floor
\[
P_+^n\{T_x=0\}+P_-^n\{T_x=1\}
\ge c_0,
\qquad c_0:=\tfrac12 e^{-C_0}>0,
\]
for every \(x\in B_n\) and all large \(n\).

Plugging this back into the regret inequality gives
\[
E_{P_+^n}R_{P_{n,+}}(\widehat\pi)+E_{P_-^n}R_{P_{n,-}}(\widehat\pi)
\ge c_0 h_n P_X(B_n).
\]
By def:two-point-witness, \(P_X(B_n)=c_B h_n^\alpha\). Therefore
\[
E_{P_+^n}R_{P_{n,+}}(\widehat\pi)+E_{P_-^n}R_{P_{n,-}}(\widehat\pi)
\ge c_0 c_B h_n^{1+\alpha}.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\max\Bigl\{E_{P_+^n}R_{P_{n,+}}(\widehat\pi),\,E_{P_-^n}R_{P_{n,-}}(\widehat\pi)\Bigr\}
\ge \frac{c_0 c_B}{2} h_n^{1+\alpha}.
\]
Because both witness laws lie in \(P_{class}(\alpha,\gamma)\),
\[
\sup_{P\in P_{class}(\alpha,\gamma)} E_P[R_P(\widehat\pi)]
\ge \frac{c_0 c_B}{2} h_n^{1+\alpha}.
\]
Taking the infimum over all measurable \(\Pi\)-valued estimators yields
\[
M_n(\alpha,\gamma)
=\inf_{\widehat\pi}\sup_{P\in P_{class}(\alpha,\gamma)} E_P[R_P(\widehat\pi)]
\ge c h_n^{1+\alpha}
\]
for some constant \(c>0\) independent of \(n\).

Finally, by def:exponents, \(D_{ag}=2+\alpha+\beta_{ag}\), \(h_n=n^{-1/D_{ag}}\), and
\[
r_\star(\alpha,\gamma)=\frac{1+\alpha}{D_{ag}}.
\]
Hence
\[
h_n^{1+\alpha}=n^{-(1+\alpha)/D_{ag}}=n^{-r_\star(\alpha,\gamma)}.
\]
Therefore, for all large \(n\),
\[
M_n(\alpha,\gamma)\ge c\,n^{-r_\star(\alpha,\gamma)}.
\]
This is the claimed minimax lower bound.

snapshot.stmt: Assume the two explicit witness-optimal policies of the corrected def:two-point-witness, namely x -> 1 and x -> 1{x notin B_n}, belong to Pi for all large n. Then M_n(alpha,gamma) = inf_{pi_hat} sup_{P in P_class} E_P R_P(pi_hat) >= c n^{-r_star(alpha,gamma)} for all large n, with c > 0 independent of n.

defs.def:minimax-regret: M_n(alpha,gamma) = inf over measurable data-dependent Pi-valued estimators pi_hat of sup_{P in P_class} E_P R_P(pi_hat).
defs.def:exponents: beta_ag = (0 if gamma = 0 else alpha gamma/(alpha+1)); D_ag = 2 + alpha + beta_ag; r_star(alpha,gamma) = (1+alpha)/D_ag.

oeq:feasible-upper.proof_tex: We prove the feasible upper bound using the corrected feasible-rate definition proposed below. Fix \(P\in P_{class}(\alpha,\gamma)\). Condition on the nuisance-training folds, so the cross-fitted nuisance estimates are fixed on the fold where the empirical policy value is evaluated. Write \(\widehat e_q\) for the clipped estimated propensity and \(b_q(x)=E_P[\Gamma_q(O;\widehat\eta)\mid X=x]-\tau_P(x)\). By lem:clip-bias, \(b_q=(\widehat e_q-e_P)(\Delta_1/\widehat e_q+\Delta_0/(1-\widehat e_q))\). Since \(q\le \widehat e_q\le 1-q\), lem:feasible-drift-global-bound gives the uniform drift scale \(B_n(q)=C\{r_{\mu,n}r_{e,n}/q+1\{\gamma>0\}r_{\mu,n}q^{1/(2\gamma)}\}\), with the last term absent at \(\gamma=0\) when \(q\le\underline p\). The ERM inequality from def:feasible-erm gives \(P_n[(\pi_\star-\widehat\pi_n)\Gamma_q(\widehat\eta)]\le0\), hence \(R_P(\widehat\pi_n)\le (P-P_n)[(\pi_\star-\widehat\pi_n)\Gamma_q(\widehat\eta)]+|P[(\pi_\star-\widehat\pi_n)b_q]|\). By ass:vc-localized-envelope and the clipped-score second-moment bound \(E_P[\Gamma_q(O;\widehat\eta)^2 1_D(X)\mid \widehat\eta]\le Cq^{-1}P_X(D)\), the localized empirical term has expectation at most \(C n^{-1/2}q^{-1/2}P_X(D_{\widehat\pi_n})^{1/2}(\log n)^{p_0}\). By thm:margin-localization, \(P_X(D_{\widehat\pi_n})^{1/2}\le C R_P(\widehat\pi_n)^{\alpha/(2+2\alpha)}\). Thus, with \(A_n(q)=n^{-1/2}q^{-1/2}(\log n)^{p_0}\), \(s=\alpha/(2+2\alpha)\), and \(\kappa=1/(1-s)=2(1+\alpha)/(2+\alpha)\), the basic inequality is \(R_P(\widehat\pi_n)\le C A_n(q)R_P(\widehat\pi_n)^s+C B_n(q)\). The elementary inequality \(r\le ar^s+b\), \(0\le s<1\), implies \(r\le C_s(a^{1/(1-s)}+b)\); applying it gives \(E_P R_P(\widehat\pi_n)\le C\{[n^{-1/2}q^{-1/2}(\log n)^{p_0}]^\kappa+r_{\mu,n}r_{e,n}/q+1\{\gamma>0\}r_{\mu,n}q^{1/(2\gamma)}\}\). This is lem:feasible-additive-master-bound. For \(\gamma>0\), assume polynomial nuisance certificates \(r_{\mu,n}=O(n^{-a})\) and \(r_{\mu,n}r_{e,n}=O(n^{-c})\) with \(c\ge1/2\). Put \(q_n=\min\{1/4,n^{-\lambda}\}\). Ignoring constants, the three displayed terms have exponents \(r_0(1-\lambda)\), \(c-\lambda\), and \(a+\lambda/(2\gamma)\), where \(r_0=(1+\alpha)/(2+\alpha)\). Therefore the optimized raw feasible exponent is \(\widetilde r_{feas}=\sup_{\lambda\ge0}\min\{r_0(1-\lambda),c-\lambda,a+\lambda/(2\gamma)\}\), with optimizer \(\lambda_\star\) given in lem:additive-clip-balance-exponent. The declared minimax-comparable exponent is \(r_{feas}=\min\{r_\star,\widetilde r_{feas}\}\). Since \(r_{feas}\le\widetilde r_{feas}\), the same estimator satisfies \(\sup_{P\in P_{class}}E_P R_P(\widehat\pi_n)\le Cn^{-r_{feas}}(\log n)^p\). Also \(r_{feas}\le r_\star\) by construction. At \(\gamma=0\), choose a fixed \(q\le\underline p\). The true clipping displacement vanishes, \(r_{\mu,n}r_{e,n}/q=O(n^{-1/2})\), and the empirical term gives exponent \(r_0=(1+\alpha)/(2+\alpha)\). Because \(\beta_{\alpha,0}=0\), \(r_\star=r_0\), so \(r_{feas}=r_\star\).

snapshot.stmt: CONSTRUCT-AND-DETERMINE. Assume gamma<=alpha+1, ass:marginal-overlap, ass:nuisance-rate, ass:vc-localized-envelope, and at gamma=0 also ass:strict-overlap-endpoint. For the plain cross-fit clipped-AIPW ERM of def:feasible-erm with clip q chosen by the analysis, DERIVE (do NOT pre-specify) the best worst-case welfare-regret exponent r_feas with sup_{P in P_class(alpha,gamma)} E_P R_P(pi_hat_n) <= C n^{-r_feas} (log n)^p, by balancing the localized variance against the estimated-nuisance CLIP BIAS over q. State the clip q and the resulting r_feas in closed form, confirm r_feas<=r_star, and reduce at gamma=0.

defs.def:feasible-erm: Vhat_{n,q}(pi) = n^{-1} sum_i pi(X_i) Gamma_q(O_i; etahat^{(-k(i))}) with K-fold cross-fitted nuisances etahat and clip q in (0,1/2]; pi_hat_n in argmax_{pi in Pi} Vhat_{n,q}(pi). The clip q is the analysis tuning parameter (chosen by the bias-variance balance).
defs.def:clipped-aipw-score: Gamma_q(O; eta) = mu_1(X) - mu_0(X) + (A/e_q(X;eta))(Y - mu_1(X)) - ((1-A)/(1-e_q(X;eta)))(Y - mu_0(X)).
defs.def:feasible-rate: Let kappa=2(1+alpha)/(2+alpha). For gamma>0 and certified polynomial nuisance exponents a,c satisfying r_mu,n=O(n^{-a}) and r_mu,n r_e,n=O(n^{-c}), c>=1/2, define zeta(a,c,gamma)=sup_{lambda>=0} min{1/2-lambda/2,c-lambda,a+lambda/(2gamma)} with the closed-form optimizer in lem:clip-balance-exponent. Define r_plug=kappa zeta(a,c,gamma), q_n=n^{-lambda_*} capped at 1/4, and r_feas=min{r_star(alpha,gamma),r_plug}. For gamma=0 under a strict-overlap floor, set q_n to a fixed value below the floor and r_feas=r_star=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha).
defs.def:law-class: { P : ass:iid, ass:consistency, ass:unconfoundedness, ass:bounded-outcome, ass:positivity, ass:margin, ass:zero-effect, ass:overlap-decay, ass:policy-class, and ass:optimal-in-class hold at exponents (alpha,gamma) }

assumptions.ass:overlap-decay: P{p_P(X) <= v, 0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u} <= C_o u^alpha v^(1/gamma) for all 0 < v <= c_o u^gamma, with v^(1/gamma)=1 when gamma=0.
assumptions.ass:marginal-overlap: P{p_P(X) <= v} <= C_om v^(1/gamma) for all 0 < v <= c_o, with the convention v^(1/gamma)=1 when gamma=0.
assumptions.ass:nuisance-rate: The cross-fitted nuisance estimators satisfy ||muhat_a-mu_a||_{L2(P)} <= r_mu_n and ||ehat-e||_{L2(P)} <= r_e_n for a in {0,1}, with product-rate r_mu_n r_e_n = O(n^{-1/2}).
assumptions.ass:margin: P(0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u) <= C_m u^alpha for all 0 < u <= u_0.
assumptions.ass:vc-localized-envelope: For the finite-VC class Pi there is a localized Rademacher/Dudley entropy-integral envelope psi with psi(r) <= C sqrt(d_Pi/n) r polylog(n) controlling the localized offset empirical processes uniformly over the class.
assumptions.ass:strict-overlap-endpoint: When gamma=0, p_P(X) >= underline_p holds P_X-almost surely, for a constant underline_p in (0,1/2].

thm:rate-characterization.proof_tex: This proves the narrowed rate-characterization statement proposed below. The lower bound is exactly thm:minimax-lower: under ass:margin-window and the policy-richness condition that the two witness-optimal policies belong to \(\Pi\), there is \(c>0\) such that \(M_n(\alpha,\gamma)\ge c n^{-r_\star(\alpha,\gamma)}\) for all large \(n\). For the upper bound, oeq:feasible-upper constructs the cross-fitted clipped-AIPW ERM with \(q_n=\min\{1/4,n^{-\lambda_\star}\}\) when \(\gamma>0\), and with fixed \(q\le\underline p\) when \(\gamma=0\), and proves \(\sup_{P\in P_{class}(\alpha,\gamma)}E_P R_P(\widehat\pi_n)\le Cn^{-r_{feas}(\alpha,\gamma)}(\log n)^p\) under ass:marginal-overlap, ass:nuisance-rate, ass:vc-localized-envelope, and the strict-overlap endpoint at \(\gamma=0\). Since \(M_n\) is the infimum over all measurable \(\Pi\)-valued estimators, it is bounded above by the risk of this particular estimator. Combining the two inequalities yields \(c n^{-r_\star}\le M_n(\alpha,\gamma)\le Cn^{-r_{feas}}(\log n)^p\). The corrected definition sets \(r_{feas}=\min\{r_\star,\widetilde r_{feas}\}\), hence \(r_{feas}\le r_\star\). At \(\gamma=0\), \(\beta_{\alpha,0}=0\) and oeq:feasible-upper gives \(r_{feas}=r_\star=(1+\alpha)/(2+\alpha)\). For \(\gamma>0\), the gap is positive exactly in the nuisance-limited subregime \(\widetilde r_{feas}<r_\star\); otherwise the high-level nuisance-rate calculation closes the bracket at the converse exponent.

snapshot.stmt: Under ass:margin-window, ass:marginal-overlap, ass:nuisance-rate, Pi rich (witness-optimal policies in Pi), gamma<=alpha+1: c n^{-r_star(alpha,gamma)} <= M_n(alpha,gamma) <= C n^{-r_feas(alpha,gamma)} (log n)^p, with r_star=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha+beta_{alpha,gamma}) (sharp converse, thm:minimax-lower, all gamma) and r_feas the DERIVED plain-clipped-AIPW exponent (oeq:feasible-upper), r_feas<=r_star with equality at gamma=0. FIRST welfare-regret rate under weak (one-sided polynomial) overlap, extending strict-overlap policy learning. The r_star-r_feas gap (positive for gamma>0) is the weak-overlap nuisance-estimation cost; tightness is open (oeq:feasible-tight).

defs.def:feasible-rate: Let kappa=2(1+alpha)/(2+alpha). For gamma>0 and certified polynomial nuisance exponents a,c satisfying r_mu,n=O(n^{-a}) and r_mu,n r_e,n=O(n^{-c}), c>=1/2, define zeta(a,c,gamma)=sup_{lambda>=0} min{1/2-lambda/2,c-lambda,a+lambda/(2gamma)} with the closed-form optimizer in lem:clip-balance-exponent. Define r_plug=kappa zeta(a,c,gamma), q_n=n^{-lambda_*} capped at 1/4, and r_feas=min{r_star(alpha,gamma),r_plug}. For gamma=0 under a strict-overlap floor, set q_n to a fixed value below the floor and r_feas=r_star=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha).
defs.def:exponents: beta_ag = (0 if gamma = 0 else alpha gamma/(alpha+1)); D_ag = 2 + alpha + beta_ag; r_star(alpha,gamma) = (1+alpha)/D_ag.

lem:clip-bias.proof_tex:
Fix a measurable plug-in nuisance triple \(\bar\eta=(\bar\mu_0,\bar\mu_1,\bar e)\), let \(\bar e_q(x)=\min\{1-q,\max\{q,\bar e(x)\}\}\), and define \(\Delta_a(x)=\bar\mu_a(x)-\mu_a(x)\). Fix \(x\in X\). Write \(e=e_P(x)\), \(\mu_a=\mu_a(x)\), \(\bar\mu_a=\bar\mu_a(x)\), and \(\bar e_q=\bar e_q(x)\). By def:clipped-aipw-score,
\[
\Gamma_q(O;\bar\eta)=\bar\mu_1(X)-\bar\mu_0(X)+\frac{A}{\bar e_q(X)}\{Y-\bar\mu_1(X)\}-\frac{1-A}{1-\bar e_q(X)}\{Y-\bar\mu_0(X)\}.
\]
Conditioning on \(X=x\) and using \(E_P[A\mid X=x]=e\), \(E_P[Y\mid A=1,X=x]=\mu_1\), and \(E_P[Y\mid A=0,X=x]=\mu_0\), we obtain
\[
E_P[\Gamma_q(O;\bar\eta)\mid X=x]
=\bar\mu_1-\bar\mu_0+\frac{e}{\bar e_q}(\mu_1-\bar\mu_1)-\frac{1-e}{1-\bar e_q}(\mu_0-\bar\mu_0).
\]
Subtract \(\tau_P(x)=\mu_1-\mu_0\). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
E_P[\Gamma_q(O;\bar\eta)\mid X=x]-\tau_P(x)
&=(\bar\mu_1-\mu_1)-(\bar\mu_0-\mu_0)-\frac{e}{\bar e_q}(\bar\mu_1-\mu_1)+\frac{1-e}{1-\bar e_q}(\bar\mu_0-\mu_0)\\
&=\Delta_1\left(1-\frac{e}{\bar e_q}\right)-\Delta_0\left(1-\frac{1-e}{1-\bar e_q}\right).
\end{aligned}
\]
Now
\[
1-\frac{e}{\bar e_q}=\frac{\bar e_q-e}{\bar e_q},
\qquad
1-\frac{1-e}{1-\bar e_q}=\frac{e-\bar e_q}{1-\bar e_q}=-\frac{\bar e_q-e}{1-\bar e_q}.
\]
Substituting these identities yields
\[
E_P[\Gamma_q(O;\bar\eta)\mid X=x]-\tau_P(x)
=(\bar e_q-e_P(x))\left(\frac{\Delta_1(x)}{\bar e_q(x)}+\frac{\Delta_0(x)}{1-\bar e_q(x)}\right).
\]
This is the claimed identity. If \(\bar e_q(x)=e_P(x)\), the prefactor vanishes. If \(\Delta_0(x)=\Delta_1(x)=0\), the bracket vanishes. But the condition \(p_P(x)>q\) alone says only that the true propensity is not clipped at level \(q\); it does not imply \(\bar e_q(x)=e_P(x)\), so it does not force cancellation. In particular, under the frozen assumptions alone one cannot replace this product-form drift by an additive clipped-region term supported only on \(\{p_P\le q\}\).

snapshot.stmt:
Exact clipped-score drift identity. For any measurable plug-in nuisance triple \(\bar\eta=(\bar\mu_0,\bar\mu_1,\bar e)\), with \(\bar e_q=\min\{1-q,\max\{q,\bar e\}\}\) and \(\Delta_a=\bar\mu_a-\mu_a\), the conditional mean drift satisfies
\[
E_P[\Gamma_q(O;\bar\eta)\mid X=x]-\tau_P(x)
=(\bar e_q(x)-e_P(x))\left(\frac{\Delta_1(x)}{\bar e_q(x)}+\frac{\Delta_0(x)}{1-\bar e_q(x)}\right).
\]
Hence cancellation holds only where \(\bar e_q(x)=e_P(x)\) or both regression errors vanish; it does not follow merely from \(p_P(x)>q\). Under the frozen assumptions alone, no additive clipped-region bound of the form asserted in the current lemma is derivable.

defs.def:clipped-aipw-score: Gamma_q(O; eta) = mu_1(X) - mu_0(X) + (A/e_q(X;eta))(Y - mu_1(X)) - ((1-A)/(1-e_q(X;eta)))(Y - mu_0(X)).

oeq:feasible-tight.proof_tex: The exact feasible tightness question is solved in all regimes where r_feas(alpha,gamma)=r_star(alpha,gamma), including gamma=0: the cross-fitted clipped-AIPW ERM attains n^{-r_star} up to logarithmic factors, and thm:minimax-lower rules out any faster polynomial exponent. In the remaining weak-overlap nuisance-limited regimes the strongest proved statement is the bracket c n^{-r_star(alpha,gamma)} <= M_n(alpha,gamma) <= sup_P E_P R_P(pi_hat_n) <= C n^{-r_feas(alpha,gamma)}(log n)^p with r_feas<r_star; whether this bracket is tight remains the isolated open problem.

snapshot.stmt: OPEN: determine the welfare-regret rate attainable by a genuinely feasible estimator, in particular the cross-fitted clipped-AIPW ERM of def:feasible-erm, under weak overlap. Can any feasible estimator attain the converse exponent r_star(alpha,gamma), or must every feasible procedure be strictly slower because the weak-arm outcome regression must be learned on the clipped region?
